title: Cassegrain reflector

infobox.value: is a combination of a primary [concave mirror](/wiki/Concave_mirror "Concave mirror") and a secondary [convex mirror](/wiki/Convex_mirror "Convex mirror"), often used in [optical telescopes](/wiki/Optical_telescope "Optical telescope") and [radio antennas](/wiki/Antenna_(radio) "Antenna (radio)"), the main characteristic being that the optical path folds back onto itself, relative to the optical system's primary mirror entrance aperture. This design puts the [focal point](/wiki/Focus_(optics) "Focus (optics)") at a convenient location behind the [primary mirror](/wiki/Primary_mirror "Primary mirror") and the convex secondary adds a [telephoto](/wiki/Telephoto_lens "Telephoto lens") effect creating a much longer [focal length](/wiki/Focal_length "Focal length") in a mechanically short system.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Combination of concave and convex mirrors

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Cassegrain.en.png/330px-Cassegrain.en.png)](/wiki/File:Cassegrain.en.png)

Light path in a Cassegrain reflecting telescope

The **Cassegrain reflector** is a combination of a primary [concave mirror](/wiki/Concave_mirror "Concave mirror") and a secondary [convex mirror](/wiki/Convex_mirror "Convex mirror"), often used in [optical telescopes](/wiki/Optical_telescope "Optical telescope") and [radio antennas](/wiki/Antenna_(radio) "Antenna (radio)"), the main characteristic being that the optical path folds back onto itself, relative to the optical system's primary mirror entrance aperture. This design puts the [focal point](/wiki/Focus_(optics) "Focus (optics)") at a convenient location behind the [primary mirror](/wiki/Primary_mirror "Primary mirror") and the convex secondary adds a [telephoto](/wiki/Telephoto_lens "Telephoto lens") effect creating a much longer [focal length](/wiki/Focal_length "Focal length") in a mechanically short system.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

In a symmetrical Cassegrain both mirrors are aligned about the [optical axis](/wiki/Optical_axis "Optical axis"), and the primary mirror usually contains a hole in the center, thus permitting the light to reach an [eyepiece](/wiki/Eyepiece "Eyepiece"), a [camera](/wiki/Camera "Camera"), or an [image sensor](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor"). Alternatively, as in many radio telescopes, the final focus may be in front of the primary. In an asymmetrical Cassegrain, the mirror(s) may be tilted to avoid obscuration of the primary or to avoid the need for a hole in the primary mirror (or both).

The classic Cassegrain configuration uses a [parabolic reflector](/wiki/Parabolic_reflector "Parabolic reflector") as the primary while the secondary mirror is [hyperbolic](/wiki/Hyperboloid "Hyperboloid").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Modern variants may have a hyperbolic primary for increased performance (for example, the [Ritchey–Chrétien design](/wiki/Ritchey%E2%80%93Chr%C3%A9tien_telescope "Ritchey–Chrétien telescope")); and either or both mirrors may be spherical or elliptical for ease of manufacturing.

The Cassegrain reflector is named after a published [reflecting telescope](/wiki/Reflecting_telescope "Reflecting telescope") design that appeared in the April 25, 1672 *[Journal des sçavans](/wiki/Journal_des_s%C3%A7avans "Journal des sçavans")* which has been attributed to [Laurent Cassegrain](/wiki/Laurent_Cassegrain "Laurent Cassegrain").[[3]](#cite_note-3) Similar designs using convex secondary mirrors have been found in the [Bonaventura Cavalieri](/wiki/Bonaventura_Cavalieri "Bonaventura Cavalieri")'s 1632 writings describing burning mirrors[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Marin Mersenne](/wiki/Marin_Mersenne "Marin Mersenne")'s 1636 writings describing telescope designs.[[6]](#cite_note-6) [James Gregory](/wiki/James_Gregory_(mathematician) "James Gregory (mathematician)")'s 1662 attempts to create a reflecting telescope included a Cassegrain configuration, judging by a convex secondary mirror found among his experiments.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The Cassegrain design is also used in [catadioptric systems](/wiki/Catadioptric_system "Catadioptric system").

## Cassegrain designs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Cassegrain designs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Cassegrain_Telescope.svg/500px-Cassegrain_Telescope.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Cassegrain_Telescope.svg)

Light path in a Cassegrain reflector telescope

### "Classic" Cassegrain telescopes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: \"Classic\" Cassegrain telescopes")]

The "classic" Cassegrain has a parabolic primary mirror and a hyperbolic secondary mirror that reflects the light back down through a hole in the primary. Folding the optics makes this a compact design. On smaller telescopes, and camera lenses, the secondary is often mounted on an optically flat, optically clear glass plate that closes the telescope tube. This support eliminates the "star-shaped" diffraction effects caused by a straight-vaned support spider. The closed tube stays clean, and the primary is protected, at the cost of some loss of light-gathering power.

It makes use of the special properties of parabolic and hyperbolic reflectors. A concave [parabolic reflector](/wiki/Parabolic_reflector "Parabolic reflector") will reflect all incoming light rays parallel to its axis of symmetry to a single point, the focus. A convex hyperbolic reflector has two foci and will reflect all light rays directed at one of its two foci towards its other focus. The mirrors in this type of telescope are designed and positioned so that they share one focus and so that the second focus of the hyperbolic mirror will be at the same point at which the image is to be observed, usually just outside the eyepiece.

In most Cassegrain systems, the secondary mirror blocks a central portion of the aperture. This ring-shaped entrance aperture significantly reduces a portion of the [modulation transfer function](/wiki/Modulation_transfer_function "Modulation transfer function") (MTF) over a range of low spatial frequencies, compared to a full-aperture design such as a refractor or an offset Cassegrain.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This MTF notch has the effect of lowering image contrast when imaging broad features. In addition, the support for the secondary (the spider) may introduce diffraction spikes in images.

The [radii of curvature](/wiki/Radius_of_curvature_(optics) "Radius of curvature (optics)") of the primary and secondary mirrors, respectively, in the classic configuration are

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{1}=-{\frac {2DF}{F-B}}=-{\frac {2F}{M}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0398bcc04e264f9ad133894bd3cf90987d0e12cf)

and

:   ![{\displaystyle R_{2}=-{\frac {2DB}{F-B-D}}=-{\frac {2B}{M-1}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9a0b45b16c42a80886adf98133e4dbdd09239550)

where

* ![{\displaystyle F}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/545fd099af8541605f7ee55f08225526be88ce57) is the effective [focal length](/wiki/Focal_length "Focal length") of the system,
* ![{\displaystyle B}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47136aad860d145f75f3eed3022df827cee94d7a) is the back focal length (the distance from the secondary to the focus),
* ![{\displaystyle D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f34a0c600395e5d4345287e21fb26efd386990e6) is the distance between the two mirrors and
* ![{\displaystyle M=(F-B)/D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/afc9cd40fa6df227bebe37ed5aa972b0358ed5db) is the secondary magnification.

If, instead of ![{\displaystyle B}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47136aad860d145f75f3eed3022df827cee94d7a) and ![{\displaystyle D}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f34a0c600395e5d4345287e21fb26efd386990e6), the known quantities are the focal length of the primary mirror, ![{\displaystyle f_{1}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/50dfd257a51e037112c917f8a9e47c9c053466df), and the distance to the focus behind the primary mirror, ![{\displaystyle b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f11423fbb2e967f986e36804a8ae4271734917c3), then ![{\displaystyle D=f_{1}(F-b)/(F+f_{1})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/98ea4ebe11c71f1ed07e3bfa648b82a16b5f42e1) and ![{\displaystyle B=D+b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/543d5c5b11d386a77800f9875e364c23a5a18b51).

The [conic constant](/wiki/Conic_constant "Conic constant") of the primary mirror is that of a parabola, ![{\displaystyle K_{1}=-1}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b031ab017ed125863e6f2a786dd38dc976a7e971). Thanks to that there is no [spherical aberration](/wiki/Spherical_aberration "Spherical aberration") introduced by the primary mirror. The secondary mirror, however, is of a hyperbolic shape with one focus coinciding with that of the primary mirror and the other focus being at the back focal length ![{\displaystyle B}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47136aad860d145f75f3eed3022df827cee94d7a). Thus, the classical Cassegrain has ideal focus for the chief ray (the center spot diagram is one point). We have,

:   ![{\displaystyle K_{2}=-1-\alpha -{\sqrt {\alpha (\alpha +2)}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/45eaeaa8832adb54eb3d0807da7cc9b143b8e326),

where

:   ![{\displaystyle \alpha ={\frac {1}{2}}\left[{\frac {4DBM}{(F+BM-DM)(F-B-D)}}\right]^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f5a3209f5771dadb7bc41adfd3bbbcb4abc88b31).

Actually, as the conic constants should not depend on scaling, the formulae for both ![{\displaystyle \alpha }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b79333175c8b3f0840bfb4ec41b8072c83ea88d3) and ![{\displaystyle K_{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/57e1b324cf5b68f2729a8634ff76e396b634b75d) can be greatly simplified and presented only as functions of the secondary magnification. Finally,

:   ![{\displaystyle \alpha ={\frac {8M^{2}}{(M^{2}-1)^{2}}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7f03ce5284610f98aed4de76bedd5b5df635dcf9)

and

:   ![{\displaystyle K_{2}=-1-{\frac {4M}{(M-1)^{2}}}=-\left({\frac {M+1}{M-1}}\right)^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/90c23b9699ba5339fab1d34b9933ac4f7d007682).

### Ritchey-Chrétien

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Ritchey-Chrétien")]

Main article: [Ritchey-Chrétien telescope](/wiki/Ritchey-Chr%C3%A9tien_telescope "Ritchey-Chrétien telescope")

The Ritchey-Chrétien is a specialized Cassegrain reflector which has two hyperbolic mirrors (instead of a parabolic primary). It is free of [coma](/wiki/Coma_(optics) "Coma (optics)") and [spherical aberration](/wiki/Spherical_aberration "Spherical aberration") at a flat focal plane, making it well suited for wide field and photographic observations. It was invented by [George Willis Ritchey](/wiki/George_Willis_Ritchey "George Willis Ritchey") and [Henri Chrétien](/wiki/Henri_Chr%C3%A9tien "Henri Chrétien") in the early 1910s. This design is very common in large professional research telescopes, including the [Hubble Space Telescope](/wiki/Hubble_Space_Telescope "Hubble Space Telescope"), the [Keck Telescopes](/wiki/Keck_Telescopes "Keck Telescopes"), and the [Very Large Telescope](/wiki/Very_Large_Telescope "Very Large Telescope") (VLT); it is also found in high-grade amateur telescopes.

### Dall-Kirkham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dall-Kirkham")]

The Dall-Kirkham Cassegrain telescope design was created by [Horace Dall](/wiki/Horace_Dall "Horace Dall") in 1928 and took on the name in an article published in *[Scientific American](/wiki/Scientific_American "Scientific American")* in 1930 following discussion between amateur astronomer Allan Kirkham and Albert G. Ingalls, the magazine's astronomy editor at the time. It uses a concave [elliptical](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") primary mirror and a convex [spherical](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere") secondary. While this system is easier to polish than a classic Cassegrain or Ritchey-Chretien system, the off-axis coma is significantly worse, so the image degrades quickly off-axis. Because this is less noticeable at longer [focal ratios](/wiki/F-number "F-number"), Dall-Kirkhams are seldom faster than f/15.

### Off-axis configurations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Off-axis configurations")]

An unusual variant of the Cassegrain is the *[Schiefspiegler](/wiki/Reflecting_telescope#Schiefspiegler "Reflecting telescope")* telescope ("skewed" or "oblique reflector"; also known as the "Kutter telescope" after its inventor, [Anton Kutter](/wiki/Anton_Kutter "Anton Kutter")[[9]](#cite_note-9)) which uses tilted mirrors to avoid the secondary mirror casting a shadow on the primary. However, while eliminating diffraction patterns this leads to several other aberrations that must be corrected.

Several different off-axis configurations are used for radio antennas.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Another off-axis, unobstructed design and variant of the Cassegrain is the '[Yolo](/wiki/Reflecting_telescope#Yolo "Reflecting telescope")' reflector invented by Arthur Leonard[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]This design uses a spherical or parabolic primary and a mechanically warped spherical secondary to correct for off-axis induced astigmatism. When set up correctly the Yolo is claimed to give unobstructed views of planetary objects and non-wide field targets, with no lack of contrast or image quality caused by spherical aberration[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. The lack of obstruction also eliminates the diffraction associated with Cassegrain and Newtonian reflector astrophotography.

## Catadioptric Cassegrains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Catadioptric Cassegrains")]

Catadioptric Cassegrains use two mirrors, often with a spherical primary mirror to reduce cost, combined with refractive corrector element(s) to correct the resulting aberrations.

### Schmidt-Cassegrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Schmidt-Cassegrain")]

Main article: [Schmidt-Cassegrain telescope](/wiki/Schmidt-Cassegrain_telescope "Schmidt-Cassegrain telescope")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Schmidt-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg/250px-Schmidt-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Schmidt-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg)

Light path in a [Schmidt-Cassegrain](/wiki/Schmidt-Cassegrain_telescope "Schmidt-Cassegrain telescope")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Maksutov-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg/250px-Maksutov-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Maksutov-Cassegrain-Telescope.svg)

Light path in a [Maksutov-Cassegrain](/wiki/Maksutov_telescope "Maksutov telescope")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Diagram_Reflector_KlevtsovCassegrain.svg/250px-Diagram_Reflector_KlevtsovCassegrain.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Diagram_Reflector_KlevtsovCassegrain.svg)

Light path in a [Klevtsov-Cassegrain telescope](/wiki/Klevtsov-Cassegrain_telescope "Klevtsov-Cassegrain telescope")

The Schmidt-Cassegrain was developed from the wide-field [Schmidt camera](/wiki/Schmidt_camera "Schmidt camera"), although the Cassegrain configuration gives it a much narrower field of view. The first optical element is a [Schmidt corrector plate](/wiki/Schmidt_corrector_plate "Schmidt corrector plate"). The plate is [figured](/wiki/Figuring "Figuring") by placing a vacuum on one side, and grinding the exact correction required to correct the [spherical aberration](/wiki/Spherical_aberration "Spherical aberration") caused by the spherical primary mirror. Schmidt-Cassegrains are popular with amateur astronomers. An early Schmidt-Cassegrain camera was patented in 1946 by artist/architect/physicist [Roger Hayward](/wiki/Roger_Hayward "Roger Hayward"),[[11]](#cite_note-11) with the film holder placed outside the telescope.

### Maksutov-Cassegrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Maksutov-Cassegrain")]

Main article: [Maksutov telescope](/wiki/Maksutov_telescope "Maksutov telescope")

The Maksutov-Cassegrain is a variation of the [Maksutov telescope](/wiki/Maksutov_telescope "Maksutov telescope") named after the [Soviet](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") [optician](/wiki/Scientific_equipment_optician "Scientific equipment optician") and [astronomer](/wiki/Astronomer "Astronomer") [Dmitri Dmitrievich Maksutov](/wiki/Dmitri_Dmitrievich_Maksutov "Dmitri Dmitrievich Maksutov"). It starts with an optically transparent corrector lens that is a section of a hollow sphere. It has a spherical primary mirror, and a spherical secondary that is usually a mirrored section of the corrector lens.

### Argunov-Cassegrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Argunov-Cassegrain")]

Main article: [Argunov-Cassegrain telescope](/wiki/Argunov-Cassegrain_telescope "Argunov-Cassegrain telescope")

In the Argunov-Cassegrain telescope all optics are spherical, and the classical Cassegrain secondary mirror is replaced by a [sub-aperture corrector](/wiki/Catadioptric_system#Sub-aperture_correctors "Catadioptric system") consisting of three air spaced lens elements. The element farthest from the primary mirror is a [Mangin mirror](/wiki/Mangin_mirror "Mangin mirror"), which acts as a secondary mirror.

### Klevtsov-Cassegrain

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Klevtsov-Cassegrain")]

Main article: [Klevtsov–Cassegrain telescope](/wiki/Klevtsov%E2%80%93Cassegrain_telescope "Klevtsov–Cassegrain telescope")

The Klevtsov-Cassegrain, like the Argunov-Cassegrain, uses a sub-aperture corrector consisting of a small meniscus lens and a Mangin mirror as its "secondary mirror".[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## Cassegrain radio antennas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cassegrain_reflector&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Cassegrain radio antennas")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/DSN_Antenna_details.jpg/250px-DSN_Antenna_details.jpg)](/wiki/File:DSN_Antenna_details.jpg)

A Cassegrain radio antenna at [GDSCC](/wiki/Goldstone_Deep_Space_Communications_Complex "Goldstone Deep Space Communications Complex")

Cassegrain designs are also utilized in satellite telecommunication [earth station](/wiki/Earth_station "Earth station") antennas and [radio telescopes](/wiki/Radio_telescope "Radio telescope"), ranging in size from 2.4 metres (8 feet) to 70 metres (200 feet). The centrally located sub-reflector serves to focus radio frequency signals in a similar fashion to optical telescopes.

An example of a cassegrain radio antenna is the 70-meter (200 foot) dish at [JPL](/wiki/Jet_Propulsion_Laboratory "Jet Propulsion Laboratory")'s [Goldstone antenna complex](/wiki/Goldstone_Deep_Space_Communications_Complex "Goldstone Deep Space Communications Complex"). For this antenna, the final focus is in front of the primary, at the top of the pedestal protruding from the mirror.
